text: swollen skin. In the pseudo-safety of our bed, you curl into yourself, eyes closed, mouth drawn in a tight line, as if these could be totems to ward off a haunting. But this is the house of my girlhood. The laughter in the night, the screaming, all of it is mine. The ghost is me. My fury and sadness and confusion bound by the walls and made manifest. “The candles will help. Like an offering,” you whisper, and I curl into you, take your warmth until you are shivering. “You read it in a book,” I say, not a question. You nod, and I trace my teeth along your almond-scented throat. I want to apologize. To tell you I never wanted this. But it would be a lie. This house is stained by all the things I swallowed down until they were more poison than secret. I never imagined myself locked behind its doors again, but my father died, and we were isolating like every other millennial couple during this fucking pandemic. It was easier to move our things out of our shitty apartment and into a place with a paid mortgage and furniture that didn’t come unassembled in a box. We’d both managed to survive downsizing, but our paychecks hadn’t been stretching far enough for years. You said the house was a sort of miracle. Prayers and incantations are the same, aren’t they? So a miracle can’t be that far from a curse. The same words in different order. You sleep in fits and starts, and I stare at the door, which I know I closed but is now standing wide open. I tell myself there is nothing standing in the hallway with fingers curled around the doorway so it might peek in at us. “I hope you can see us,” I whisper to the thing I’m stupidly pretending isn’t there, hoping I can offer it a light to move into. That in seeing us together, it can finally move on. Its anger leaking back into the floors, the walls, the doors as it finally escapes into a peace it had never known. It flees, a darker smear against the night shadows. I turn back to you, and you press against me, and I would absorb you if I could. The ghost in the hall understands. She knows what it is to ache from a loss that hasn’t happened yet. To sharpen her teeth even as she longs for a softness that never existed in this house. In the morning, your eyes are bloodshot as you down your coffee and boot up for another day of pointless Zoom meetings, and I am guilty of being the wolf at your door. I lured you here with false promises of domestic bliss and big eyes and ears and teeth. But there is no rest in this house, and I know I’m to blame for it all. I rub your shoulders as a kind of weak apology, and you shrug me off. Your meeting starts in a few minutes, and you need to go through your email. I tell myself you are tired and frightened, but I am alone even with you here. I should be working too, contributing to our family unit, but I drift through the rooms, scratch at the walls until my fingers are numb. There is no ghost in the plaster, but I see it in the periphery, can hear it sobbing even with the candles we put in the room, even with it seeing us together. Your headphones are in, but your shoulders are stiff, and I know you hear it too. Your candles didn’t work. Houses aren’t supposed to be haunted in the daylight, but this one is. This is the point where we abandon everything before the sighs manifest into raised, burning scratches along our backs, but there is death outside, and we have nowhere else to go. I hear the girl version of myself crying while I am making lunch. Somewhere, the phantasm of my father is shouting at her, his hand gripping her collar as he pours out his hatred and knowledge of sin. If I close my eyes, I can feel how he bleached out my heart little by little. “Tuna?” I ask, and your eyes are large. Wet. You heard it, too, but you don’t know the sound of my girl voice yet. I clench the butter knife in my hand as if it could do anything other than scrape. “It didn’t work. The candles,” you say, and I set down the knife because I can’t trust my own hands. Not in this house. “Maybe you read it wrong,” I say. You shake your head, and your hair is all shimmering gold tipped in rose. “We should use salt. At the doors.” I turn and pretend to be putting away the bread. It won’t work. My ghost drowned in salt, her face tear-slick even as she pressed fingers to the veins at her wrist, wondering if she could ever be strong enough to open herself up and let the pain stream out of her. Upstairs a door slams, and you jump and then laugh. “This is the point we’re supposed to call in a priest, right?” “Maybe they can wear one of those masks. Those plague doctor ones. Seems only appropriate.” You shiver, and I wrap you up, your breath hot against my collarbone. “I’ll sell it once this is all over. Back to normal.” It’s a lie, but you sigh. “I don’t think normal is a thing anymore.” You drift back to your laptop, to your headphones. You pick at the sandwich I make you, and I eventually throw it away. Another thing wasted by this house. The house is quiet for the rest of the day, but there is a subtle heaviness to the air. As if the settling dust has taken on weight, burying itself in our lungs until we are left coughing blood and struggling to breathe.
